coredata.dc:title: Efficient photocatalytic degradation of emerging ciprofloxacin under visible light irradiation using BiOBr/carbon quantum dot/saponite composite
coredata.dc:creator: Chuaicham, Chitiphon
Sekar, Karthikeyan
Balakumar, Vellaichamy
Uchida, Junya
Katsurao, Takumi
Sakabe, Hiroshi
Ohtani, Bunsho
Sasaki, Keiko
coredata.dc:description: The use of visible-driven photocatalysts has fascinated attention as a capable and sustainable approach for wastewater remediation. In this work, BiOBr/carbon quantum dot (CQDs)/saponite composites (CQDs/Clay@BiOBr) were fabricated via hydrothermally using two different CQDs/Clay precursors (in-situ synthesis (IS) and physical mixing (PM)). The obtained products were characterized, and the photocatalytic performances of the prepared samples were evaluated in the photocatalytic decomposition of emerging ciprofloxacin (CIP) pharmaceutical waste. The highest CIP mineralization performance was achieved when a combination of BiOBr and CQDs/Clay (IS) with the appropriate proportion because the strong adhesion between CQDs and clay generate a great heterojunction in the composite. The stronger interaction of CQDs and better distribution of CQDs on the surface of clay in the CQDs/Clay (IS) enhanced the interaction of BiOBr and CQDs, and avoided the re-agglomeration of excess of CQDs on surface of BiOBr which reduce the active surface to receive the light and react with CIP. The ultrafast degradation rate of the optimized CQDs/Clay@BiOBr composite was better compared to others. The significant improvement in the CIP degradation efficiency of the CQDs/Clay@BiOBr composite was attributed to the excellent separation and transportation of photogenerated electrons and holes, as confirmed by photoluminescence, photocurrent density, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy results. Moreover, the photocatalytic degradation mechanism of CIP in the CQDs/Clay@BiOBr composite was proposed based on the electronic states of each material in the composite and on a scavenger test. Thus, the proposed CQDs/Clay@BiOBr composite can be employed as a potential visible-light-driven photocatalyst for the decomposition of organic contaminants in wastewater.

head.ce:title: Efficient photocatalytic degradation of emerging ciprofloxacin under visible light irradiation using BiOBr/carbon quantum dot/saponite composite

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The use of visible-driven photocatalysts has fascinated attention as a capable and sustainable approach for wastewater remediation. In this work, BiOBr/carbon quantum dot (CQDs)/saponite composites (CQDs/Clay@BiOBr) were fabricated via hydrothermally using two different CQDs/Clay precursors (in-situ synthesis (IS) and physical mixing (PM)). The obtained products were characterized, and the photocatalytic performances of the prepared samples were evaluated in the photocatalytic decomposition of emerging ciprofloxacin (CIP) pharmaceutical waste. The highest CIP mineralization performance was achieved when a combination of BiOBr and CQDs/Clay (IS) with the appropriate proportion because the strong adhesion between CQDs and clay generate a great heterojunction in the composite. The stronger interaction of CQDs and better distribution of CQDs on the surface of clay in the CQDs/Clay (IS) enhanced the interaction of BiOBr and CQDs, and avoided the re-agglomeration of excess of CQDs on surface of BiOBr which reduce the active surface to receive the light and react with CIP. The ultrafast degradation rate of the optimized CQDs/Clay@BiOBr composite was better compared to others. The significant improvement in the CIP degradation efficiency of the CQDs/Clay@BiOBr composite was attributed to the excellent separation and transportation of photogenerated electrons and holes, as confirmed by photoluminescence, photocurrent density, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy results. Moreover, the photocatalytic degradation mechanism of CIP in the CQDs/Clay@BiOBr composite was proposed based on the electronic states of each material in the composite and on a scavenger test. Thus, the proposed CQDs/Clay@BiOBr composite can be employed as a potential visible-light-driven photocatalyst for the decomposition of organic contaminants in wastewater.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalysis is considered as a promising technology for alternative energy and environmental remediation as it uses solar light, which is a sustainable energy source (Balakumar et al., 2021a, 2021c; Chuaicham et al., 2019, 2021a; Fotiou et al., 2016; Li et al., 2019; Mirzaei et al., 2018; Sekar et al., 2021b). Recently, two-dimensional (2D) photocatalysts have attracted significant attention as they provide high surface area, thereby resulting in high light absorption (Abbasi et al., 2019; Chuaicham et al., 2022c). They also provide a large contact area for other normal catalysts morphology (Gunjakar et al., 2013; Srikhaow et al., 2016). Among the 2D visible-light-driven photocatalysts, 2D bismuth oxyhalides are used as photocatalysts in organic wastewater treatment owing to their strong photo-response in the visible-light region, high photocatalytic performance, and ease of preparation (Bavani et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2022; Shi et al., 2020; Tan et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2019). Bismuth oxybromide (BiOBr), in particular, is an attractive photocatalyst owing to its narrow bandgap energy of approximately 2.7 eV, which can be used under solar-light irradiation (Du et al., 2021; Gan et al., 2020; Qin et al., 2022; Wu et al., 2021). However, practical application of pristine BiOBr is constrained due to high photogenerated charge carrier recombination, resulting in its decreased activity (Cao et al., 2019). Thus far, several modifications of BiOBr have been reported, such as structural design, element doping, and heterostructure constructions, which enhance charge carrier separation. Recently, Majhi et al. reported a novel three combination of CdS/BiOBr/Bi2O2CO3 with excellent photocatalytic activity for the atrazine decomposition (Majhi et al., 2020). Guo et al. found that the depressed recombination of the photogenerated charge carriers of BiOBr photocatalysts can be achieved by doping with Zn atoms, and the doped sample exhibited visible-light photocatalytic water splitting and degradation of organic dye (Guo et al., 2019). Kanagaraj et al. (Fu et al., 2017) reported that the SrTiO3–BiOBr heterojunction significantly enhanced the photocatalytic degradation of reactive dyes as compared to pure BiOBr (Kanagaraj and Thiripuranthagan, 2017). Further, Wei et al. reported that an in-situ Bi-decorated BiOBr photocatalyst enhanced the photocatalytic treatment of multiple antibiotics in wastewater (Cao et al., 2019). Focusing on the formation heterojunction, the use of semiconductor photocatalysts in combination with metal nanoparticles, other semiconductors to form heterojunctions or Z-scheme materials, and carbon materials to achieve superior photocatalytic performance while overcoming the limitations of using the catalysts in their pristine or individual form. For example, combining a semiconductor material with a metal nanoparticle (Scheme 1 a) or other semiconductor (Scheme 1b) are the most often utilized techniques for promoting charge separation and suppressing charge recombination (Chuaicham et al., 2022a; Li et al., 2013; Xu et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2021). Metal nanoparticles may cause Schottky-type contact junctions to develop at the semiconductor-metal nanoparticle interface, allowing charges to flow in the appropriate direction, while the combination of two semiconductors generate the interfacial heterojunction such as staggered gap (type-II), resulting to enhancing the separation of photogenerated charge carrier. Also, Z-scheme based photocatalysts made by rational integration of two narrow-band-gap semiconductors may effectively separate the photogenerated charge carriers while preserving robust redox characteristics and a wider spectrum of solar light harvesting (Jin et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2018b). The structure and charge carrier transfer route Z-scheme systems are comparable to staggered gap heterojunction photocatalysts, however the charge carrier migration mechanism is different because the charge carrier migration pattern of the Z-scheme that resembles the letter “Z” (Scheme 1c). In addition, the use of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as photosensitizers or cocatalysts in semiconductor photocatalysts is gaining popularity (Scheme 1d). When exposed to UV light, CQDs can act as electron reservoirs, preventing charge carrier recombination in the semiconductor. However, when exposed to visible light, the CQDs acted as photosensitizers, moving photogenerated electrons to the semiconductor's conduction band and driving photocatalytic activities (Zhao et al., 2017).

Recently, there has emerged an increasing interest in using carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in nanocomposites. CQDs are carbon nanoparticles with particle sizes less than 10 nm. CQDs exhibit high luminescence, high stability, and good conductivity and are low-cost materials (Gai et al., 2019; Wang and Hu, 2014; Zhao and Chung, 2018). They are employed in many applications, particularly photocatalysis, owing to their enhanced visible-light absorption ability and charge separation (Dong et al., 2021; Ji et al., 2018; Niu et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2018a). Liu et al. reported the synthesis of CQDs-modified and chloride-doped C3N4 that exhibits high hydrogen evolution (Liu et al., 2020). Bai et al. synthesized a CQDs-decorated CeO2/BiOCl composite that exhibited enhanced visible-light photoactivity toward reduction of hexavalent chromium and degradation of rhodamine B (Bai et al., 2021). However, the agglomeration of the CQDs particles deteriorated its optical properties of itself. Thus, some templates, for example silica microspheres or polymer, were used to improve the optical properties (Kwon et al., 2014; Zhao et al., 2020).

Clay minerals can be a good support for CQDs due to their high specific surface area, high chemical stability, high charge density, and promising 2D structure (Khankhasaeva and Badmaeva, 2020), resulting in decreasing the agglomeration of CQDs particle and enhancing the optical property and stability of CQDs. Saponite (Ca0.25(Mg,Fe)3 ((Si,Al)4O10) (OH)2·n (H2O)) belongs to smectite group, that is 2:1 type clay, and can be characterized by high solubility in sulfuric acid (Sparks, 2003; Vogels et al., 2005). Moreover, saponite have high purity compared with other clay minerals such as montmorillonite, kaolinite. Thus, the present work discusses the synthesis of BiOBr/CQDs/saponite composites (CQDs/Clay@BiOBr) using two different CQDs/saponite (CQDs/Clay) precursors, one prepared through in-situ pyrolysis synthesis and the other through a physical mixing process, to understand the effect of CQDs arrangement in the CQDs/saponite composite. The selection of saponite for fabrication of CQDs due to its high purity which has no effect for optical properties of CQDs product. Moreover, the content of CQDs/Clay loading in the composites was optimized to obtain the best photocatalytic performance. The composites were characterized by several techniques. Subsequently, the separation and transfer of photogenerated charge carriers of the prepared samples were investigated. Then, the photocatalytic activity of the synthesized composites was examined and compared to that of pristine BiOBr by toward the degradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP), which belongs to the fluoroquinolone class of antibiotics and is extensively used to treat bacterial infections, is one such developing antibiotic contaminant. Its concentrations in wastewaters can reach 31 mg/L or more from medication manufacturing facility effluents. At concentrations of 17–203 g/L, it inhibits big freshwater producers such as duckweed (Lemna minor) and cyanobacteria (Microcystis aeruginosa). In addition, 18–199 g/L of CIP reduced the metabolic activities of a periphyton community (Gupta et al., 2021; Kumar et al., 2022; Patel et al., 2021; Yao et al., 2021).

Bismuth (III) nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)2∙5H2O), potassium bromide (KBr), para-benzoquinone (BQ), isopropyl alcohol (IPA), and ethylenediaminetetracetic acid disodium (EDTA-2Na) were obtained from Wako Chemicals (Osaka, Japan). Saponite was supplied by Maze, Nigata, Japan. Ciprofloxacin (CIP) was purchased from Funakoshi, Japan.
In this experiment, CQDs/Clay precursors were prepared using two different methods—in-situ pyrolysis synthesis and physical mixing process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs/Clay precursor by in-situ pyrolysis synthesis (CQDs/Clay(IS))
ce:section.ce:para: Here, 1.6 g of phloroglucinol dehydrate (C6H10O5) and 0.2 g of dicyandiamide (C2H4N4) were mixed with 4 g of saponite (clay) as a template. The mixture was heated at 200 °C for 3 h in a N2 atmosphere using oil bath (FWB-180), Tokyo Garasu Kikai Co., Ltd. Then, the obtained product was washed with acetone and methanol, followed by drying under a reduced pressure system which has a temperature at 75 °C and pressure below 133 Pa using VOS-310C, Tokyo Rikakikai Co., Ltd. Through thermogravimetric analysis, the amount of CQDs in the composite was estimated to be approximately 13 wt%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs/Clay precursor by physical mixing process(CQDs/Clay(PM))
ce:section.ce:para: A mixture of 2.4 g of phloroglucinol dehydrate and 0.3 g of dicyandiamide was heated at 200 °C for 3 h in a N2 atmosphere to obtain CQDs. Then, the obtained product was washed with acetone and methanol, followed by drying under the reduced pressure system. The 1.3 g of CQDs thus obtained after drying was mixed with 8.7 g of saponite (clay) to obtain 13% of the CQD/Clay precursor through the physical mixing process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of BiOBr, BiOBr/saponite (Clay@BiOBr), and BiOBr/CQDs/saponite composites (CQDs/Clay@BiOBr)
ce:section.ce:para: BiOBr/CQDs/saponite composites were prepared using the hydrothermal method. Briefly, 0.89 g of KBr and a certain amount of CQDs/Clay (IS) (25–100 mg), obtained from the in-situ pyrolysis synthesis (IS) or physical mixing method (PM), was added into 50 mL of deionized water, followed by sonication for 30 min. Then, 2.42 g Bi(NO3)3∙5H2O was added to this suspension. The suspension was stirred for 30 min and aging via a hydrothermal method at 160 °C for 2 h. The products were collected and washed. The composite fabricated using the CQDs/Clay (IS) precursor obtained from the in-situ pyrolysis synthesis is denoted as CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-x, where x is the added amount of CQD/Clay (IS) in milligrams. Pure BiOBr, BiOBr/saponite, and BiOBr/CQDs/saponite were also prepared using only clay or CQDs/Clay (PM) under the same conditions mentioned above; these are denoted as BiOBr, Clay@BiOBr, and CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50, respectively (Scheme 2 ).

The crystal phase structures of all samples were detected utilizing an X-ray diffraction (XRD) Ultima IV diffractometer (RIGAKU, Akishima, Japan). The morphology and surface properties of all samples were confirmed by SEM (VE-9800, Keyence, Osaka, Japan) and TEM (JEM-2100HCKM, JEOL, Akishima, Japan), respectively. The light absorption ability of the pristine and composite samples was recognized by UV-DRS (UV-2450 Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). The oxidation states of the Bi and Br in samples were measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (ESCA 5800; ULVAC-PHI, Inc., Kanagawa, Japan). Photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) was achieved by an FP-6600 spectrofluorometer (FP-6600 spectrofluorometer, JASCO Corporation, Tokyo, Japan). Then, energy-resolved distribution of electron trap (ERDT) patterns obtained from reversed double-beam photoacoustic spectroscopy (RDB-PAS) for all samples were obtained. The apparatus was originally composed by Institute of Catalysis, Hokkaido University. The liquid chromatography with quadrupole time-of-flight mass spectrometry (LCMS, Agilent Technologies, UPLC/Q-TOF-MS, Santa Clara, California, USA) technique was used to detected degraded by-products. Transient photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement were conducted using 1280c AMETEK advanced measurement technology, Berkshire, United Kingdom.

The photocatalytic performances of the synthesized materials were evaluated through CIP decomposition under visible light illumination (500 W Xe lamp, > 400 nm with light intensity around 83 mW/cm2). Briefly, 50 mg of synthesized samples was putted to 50 mL of 10 ppm CIP solution (pH = 6.4). The suspended solution was keep stirring in dark for 30 min to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium. After that, the suspension was then irradiated for the photocatalytic reaction. The reaction temperature was maintaining at 25 °C by cooling jacket. At the intervals of time, 1.0 mL of the solution was taken to determine the concentration of target CIP using HPLC, column = C18 column, mobile phase = a mixture solution of 20% of 5 mM CH3COONa, 15% acetonitrile, and 65% 2% v/v acetic acid. Other photocatalytic parameters, such as catalyst loading, were also determined. Moreover, the contributions of active species, O2 −, ∙OH, and h+, were separately estimated by each scavenger.

The bio-toxicity after photocatalytic decomposition of degraded CIP using CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 after 5 min of reaction was estimated from the formation of colonies of Escherichia coli (E. coli) in the degraded solution compared with water as a control. E. coli is a representative bacterium in environments and can be commonly used as an indicator of biotoxicity tests. In the experimental detail was modified from our previous work (Chuaicham et al., 2021a), the E. coli with the bacterial suspension density around 103 colony forming units (cfu)/mL was mixed in 1:1 v/v with water, 10 ppm CIP or degraded CIP solution after photocatalytic treatment using CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 after 5 min of reaction. The 1 mL of the mixture solution was transferred and add dropped on a food MC-media pad surface (JNC Corporation, Kagoshima, Japan). Then, the above pad with the mixture sample was incubated at 37 °C for 24 h to grow the E. coli. Colonies of E. coli appear with blue dots on the MC-media a pad, depending on the biotoxicity of tested solutions.

Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of BiOBr, Clay@BiOBr, CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50, and CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr. There are main diffraction peaks at 10.8°, 22.0°, 31.6°, 32.2°, 33.1°, and 44.7°, which can be indexed to (001), (002), (102), (110), (003), and (200), respectively, of the crystal phase structure of BiOBr with the space group P4/nmm, PDF 00-009-0393 (Wu et al., 2016). The appearance of the diffraction peaks at 10.8° in the Clay@BiOBr, CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50, and CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr composites indicate appropriate devolvement of the BiOBr sheet in the presence of clay and CQDs/clay, confirming that the addition of clay, CQDs/Clay (IS) and CQDs/Clay (PM) have no effect for the formation of BiOBr. However, the crystallographic and phase structures of saponite and CQDs (Fig. S1) were not observed in the Clay@BiOBr, CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50, and CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr composites due to the low content of clay and CQDs in the composites and low crystallinity of saponite and CQDs compared with BiOBr (Fig. S1). In comparison, CQDs/Clay (IS) showed lower characteristic peaks intensity of clay than CQDs/Clay (PM) and pristine clay, suggesting some CQDs particles may doped on the surface of clay via in-situ synthesis method, resulting to reducing the crystallinity of backbone structure of pristine clay. Thus, regrading to the low content and low crystallinity of the CQDs/Clay due to doping of the CQDs on the surface clay are caused disappearing of the CQDs/Clay peaks in the CQDs/Clay@BiOBr composites.

UV-DRS technique was carried out to examine the optical properties of the materials, such as light-harvesting nature and energy bandgap, which are key factors in determining the photocatalytic activity, as shown in Fig. 2 a. BiOBr and the composites exhibit absorption edge at 380–425 nm, which indicates that these samples are well responsive to UV and visible light. The optical properties of BiOBr and Clay@BiOBr are similar; however, the light absorption ability of the composites significantly increased and the corresponding peak was broadening in the visible-light region with the increase in the amount of CQDs. This indicates that the presence of the CQDs in the CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50 and CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr composites can enhance visible-light harvesting due to the high visible-light absorption ability of the CQDs (Hu et al., 2018). Moreover, CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50 exhibited a slight enhancement of light absorption ability than CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50, indicating that CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50 may consist of a higher amount of CQDs particles on the surface of BiOBr than CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50. The large accumulation of CQDs particles on the surface of CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50 is owing to the re-adsorption of the loosed CQDs particles from CQDs/Clay (PM) as a result of decreased interactions between CQDs and clay via physical mixing simply. Therefore, the enhancement of the visible-light absorption in the CQDs composites may be effective in generating photogenerated charge carriers for the photocatalytic reaction. The bandgap energies (E
g ) of the all samples were estimated using Tauc's equation (Balakumar et al., 2021b; Chuaicham et al., 2021a). As shown in Fig. 2b, the E
g values of BiOBr and the Clay@BiOBr, CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-25, CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50, CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-100, and CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50 composites were determined as 2.96, 3.00, 3.01, 3.01, 3.05, and 3.02 eV, respectively. All the composites show slightly higher E
g values compared to that of pure BiOBr because the composites contain saponite, which has a less light absorption ability and large E
g value (Fig. S2a, b). The E
g values of the composites increase with increasing amount of the CQDs/Clay (IS) in the composite. The increasing energy band gap could enhance the separation of electron–hole pairs, leading to the enhanced photocatalytic activities of the composites (Chen et al., 2021). In addition, it was not observed if there is the significant difference in Eg of sample from different preparation methods (CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 and the CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50).

Fig. 3 shows the SEM images of BiOBr, Clay@BiOBr, CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50 and CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr under two magnifications with scale bars of 5.0 μm and 1.0 μm. It can be observed from Fig. 3a that pure BiOBr has a small plate-like structure with a smooth surface (Fig. 3a), while saponite particles are in a large aggelegated morphologies with rouged surfaces (Fig. 3b). In Fig. 3c–g, the Clay@BiOBr, CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50 and CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr composites exhibit small particles of BiOBr on the rouged surfaces of the agglegated structure of saponite, which confirms the formation of BiOBr on the surface of CQDs/Clay as the supporting material during the hydrothermal synthesis. Compared between the product from different CQDs/Clay sources, CQDs/Clay (IS) and CQDs/Clay (PM), (Fig. 3e and f), CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 show better crystallization and formation of BiOBr on saponite than CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50, indicating that utilization of CQDs/Clay (IS) as a template for the in-synthetic method may not enhance the development of plate like structure of BiOBr, however the CQDs/Clay (PM) precursor which has low interaction with the each two phase of CQDs and saponite to disturb the crystallization of BiOBr.

The effect of fabrication methods the CQDs/Clay from insitu-synthesis and physical mixing for the formation of the CQDs/Clay@BiOBr composite on the morphologies was further confirmed by the TEM observations, as shown in Fig. 4 . A plate-like structure can be observed in BiOBr and the BiOBr composite (Fig. 4a–c). Interestingly, CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50 shows many nano-dot particles on the surface of BiOBr (Fig. 4c) compared to CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 (Fig. 4b), suggesting that the CQDs from the CQDs/Clay (PM) may have less interactions with the clay. These results suggest that the in-situ synthesis method of CQDs/Clay (IS) could provide stronger interaction of CQDs on the surface of clay than the obtained CQDs/Clay (PM) from physical mixing method because the mixing of raw precursor of CQDs before calcination for the in-situ synthesis method provide a strongly charge interaction of the precursor and surface of clay, leading to generation of well interaction and arrangement of on the surface of clay. Furthermore, for XRD results in Fig. S1, the main characteristic peaks of clay in the CQDs/Clay (IS) was significantly decrease compare with the CQDs/Clay (PM) and pristine clay, suggesting the crystal distortion of clay due to high interaction of CQDs and CQDs doping on the surface of CQDs/Clay (IS). Moreover, some CQDs which may not touch with clay in CQDs/Clay (PM) are returned onto the surface of BiOBr during the synthesis of CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50, while CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 seem to show reduced amount of the CQDs on BiOBr. Thus, the difference in locations and dispersions of the CQDs on CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50 and CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 may affect the photocatalytic activity during the CIP degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy-resolved distribution of electron trap (ERDT) patterns
ce:section.ce:para: The surface electronic properties of BiOBr, Clay@BiOBr, CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50, and CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr were examined by RDB-PAS. Fig. 5 shows the energy-resolved distribution of electron traps combined with conduction band bottom (ERDT/CBB) patterns of BiOBr, Clay@BiOBr, CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50, and CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr that are plotted as a function of energy from the valence bond top (VBT) vs. electron-trap density, which can replicate the surface electronic properties of a photocatalyst with relation to photocatalytic activity (Chuaicham et al., 2020a, 2020b). It can be seen that BiOBr shows a maximum peak of electron accumulation density at approximately 2.9 eV, which is close to the CBB. This indicates that the n-type characteristic of BiOBr via the electron-trap state near the CBB. However, the characteristic pattern of the electron trap states of BiOBr shifted to a higher energy level when BiOBr was modified with saponite or CQD/clay in the composite samples. It can be clearly seen that when the amount of CQDs increases, a large shift in the electron accumulation pattern of BiOBr to upper sides was observed, thereby suggesting the distortion of the BiOBr surface by the accumulation of CQDs on the surface (Chuaicham et al., 2021b). The ERDT pattern of CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50 exhibits a large shift to higher energy level than CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50, thus implying that the surface of BiOBr was distorted more significantly in CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50 than in CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50. This might be a reason for the re-adsorption of the CQDs on the BiOBr surface in CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50, which is consistent with the TEM observation results.

The photocatalytic activities of BiOBr, Clay@BiOBr, CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50, and CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr were evaluated against the degradation of CIP, which is one of antibiotics and often found in pharmaceutical wastes, under visible light irradiation. In Fig. 6 a, all samples showed CIP adsorption of approximately 10–20% on their surfaces after 5 min under the dark reaction. After turning on the light source, the change in photocatalytic activity for the CIP degradation can be clearly observed in all samples. The CIP degradation efficiencies (C/C0) of BiOBr, Clay@BiOBr, CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-25, CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50, CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-100, and CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50 are 0.44, 0.39, 0.27, 0.14, 0.21, and 0.32, respectively.

The effects of catalyst loading and CIP concentration were also studied, and the results are presented in Fig. 6c and d, respectively. In Fig. 6c, it can be seen that the increasing the CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 content from 0.25 to 1.0 g/L resulted in increasing the adsorption of CIP in the dark condition and providing faster CIP degradation because of interaction of the surface active photocatalyst and CIP molecules. However, the photocatalytic efficiency and adsorption of CIP decrease with the increasing CIP concentration from 0.005 mM to 0.03 mM (Fig. 6d). Table 1 summarizes the photocatalytic performances of various photocatalysts for CIP degradation. It can be seen that CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 from the present study exhibits the most efficient and fastest photocatalytic performance for CIP degradation compared to other photocatalysts from previous studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of active radical and hole species
ce:section.ce:para: The radical scavenger experiments were conducted for the photocatalytic degradation of CIP by CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 to elucidate the importance of generated active radical species in the photocatalytic reaction. IPA, EDTA-2Na, and BQ were used as •OH trappings, h+, and •O2 − scavengers, respectively (Shah et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. 6e, the presence of BQ obviously inhibited the CIP degradation, thus demonstrating that the predominant radical species for the photocatalytic CIP degradation is •O2 −. Moreover, the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 for the CIP degradation decreased with the addition of IPA and EDTA-2Na, thus suggesting that h+ and •OH participated in CIP degradation as minor active species.

The reusability of CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 for the CIP degradation was performed for five cycles to analyze the recyclability of this material, which is one of the important properties of a photocatalyst. In Fig. 6f, it can be seen that the photocatalytic decomposition of CIP by CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 is more than 90% for five cycles, thus indicating that CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 has high stability and reusability for photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants in wastewater. Moreover, the stability of CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50, which exhibited the highest photocatalytic performance as long as investigated here, was determined by comparing the XRD patterns before and after the first cycle of the photocatalytic degradation of CIP. As depicted in Fig. 7 , the characteristic diffraction peaks of BiOBr still remain in the XRD patterns of CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50-spent, thereby indicating that the BiOBr structure is not destroyed during the photocatalytic removal of CIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Verification of charge transfer and separation
ce:section.ce:para: To clarify the recombination and transfer of electron, PL, transient photocurrent response measurements, and EIS were elaborated. Fig. 8 a shows the PL spectra of BiOBr, Clay@BiOBr, CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50, and CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50 in the emission wavelength range of 420–580 nm. Generally, a lower recombination rate of electron–hole pairs would result in a lower PL intensity (Karthikeyan et al., 2019; Sekar et al., 2021a). It can be seen that the composite samples—Clay@BiOBr, CQD/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50, and CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50—show lower PL emission intensity at approximately 440 nm for a 370-nm excitation wavelength than BiOBr, and the lowest PL intensity is observed for CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50. These results indicate that the heterojunction between the clay and BiOBr can suppress the recombination of electron–hole pairs. Moreover, the inhibition of the recombination was further improved by the addition of CQDs into the composite because it can accept electron and enhance the charge transfer. The excellent charge separation efficiency of CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 compared to pure BiOBr, Clay@BiOBr, and CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50 was further investigated by transient photocurrent responsive performance, and the results are shown in Fig. 8b. In previous works, there are many report the relationship between the photocurrent density and photocatalytic activity. The high photocurrent density of photocatalyst sample show high photocatalytic activity due to large generation and separation of photogenerated electron under light irradiation (Zhong et al., 2022). Indeed, CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 exhibits the highest photocurrent density than among them, thereby demonstrating that CQD/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 is the optimized composite that can provide the most efficient charge separation. Furthermore, the transfer and separation rates of the electron–hole pairs of the prepared samples were analyzed using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements. Basically, the smaller radius of the EIS Nyquist diagram implies lower charge transfer resistance. In Fig. 8c, Clay@BiOBr presents the smallest arc radius than among the investigated here, and the introduction of CQDs into Clay@BiOBr results in an apparent decline in the arc radius of the semi-circular Nyquist diagram. Compared between CQD/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 and CQD/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50, utilized CQDs/Clay (IS) from in-situ synthesis provides lower semi-circular Nyquist diagram, thereby implying the fastest electron–hole separation and transfer. Overall, the results from PL spectroscopy, transient photocurrent responsive measurements, and EIS confirmed the modification and improvement of electronic properties in CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50. Moreover, the clay can enhance the separation of the photogenerated charge carriers, particularly for the ternary combinations of clay, CQDs and BiOBr in the CQD/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 sample, resulting in further production of reactive species that improves the photocatalytic activity.

The oxidation states of Bi, Br, and O elements in BiOBr, Clay@BiOBr, CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50, and CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50, and the valence band (VB) positions of clay and BiOBr were analyzed through XPS. Fig. 9 a shows the Bi 4f spectra of all samples. The main peak at E B [Bi 3d7/2] is observed at approximately 159.4 eV in all samples; this peak can be ascribed to Bi3+ (Xia and Lo, 2016). Moreover, Fig. 9b, the oxidation state of Br in all samples was −1, as confirmed by E B [Br 3d5/2] = 68.4 eV (Liu et al., 2021). The similarity of oxidation states of Bi, and Br in the composites compared to pure BiOBr suggests that the introduction of CQDs/Clay (IS) and CQDs/Clay (PM) in the composite does not affect the chemical properties, such as the oxidation states of the main elements, of BiOBr, which is the main photocatalyst. In addition, the VB positions of clay and BiOBr were also investigated through XPS, and the VB of saponite and BiOBr were estimated to 2.77 eV and 1.64 eV, respectively, as shown in Fig. 9c and Fig. S2c. Based on the UV-DRS, the position of conduction band (CB) of saponite and BiOBr were −1.2 eV and −1.28 eV, respectively.

It is clear that the HPLC spectrum of degraded CIP solution after 5 min of reaction over CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 exhibited the decreasing of CIP peak intensity and appering several new peaks as shown Fig. S3. Thus, the products of the photocatalytic CIP degradation using CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 after 5 min of reaction were determined by LC-MS to propose the pathways of the CIP mineralization, as shown in Scheme 3 and Fig. S4. Form the results of LC-MS, it can be seen that the degraded solution of CIP, after the photocatalytic reaction, contains several smaller molecules such as m/z = 245, 219, 217, 201, 302, and 231, which are the fragments of the CIP structure. This confirms the damage of the main piperazine ring of CIP molecule, followed by the removal of –CO groups, decarboxylation and, defluorination and decarbonylation (Chuaicham et al., 2022b; Giri and Golder, 2019; Silva et al., 2016; Wen et al., 2018). Therefore, the presence of the fragments of the CIP structure confirms the distortion of CIP. In addition, to further confirm the degradation of CIP molecule by CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50, the adsorbed degradation products on the surface of spent CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 after the photocatalytic CIP degradation was checked using FTIR analysis. Fig. S5 showed the FTIR spectra of fresh CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 and spent CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 after the photocatalytic CIP degradation. It can be seen that the both samples display the characteristic vibrational peaks of Bi–O stretching mode at 510 cm−1, O–H stretching mode at 1390 and 3403 cm−1, O–H bending mode at 1632 cm−1, which are come from BiOBr phase in the composite. Also, the CC stretching mode from CQDs in the composites displays at 1632 cm−1. Interestingly, the C–C stretching mode at 2907 cm−1 was observed in only the spent CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50, suggesting some degraded CIP molecules were adsorbed after the photocatalytic treatment. Thus, this results further confirmed the de-structure of CIP via the photocatalytic process using CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50.

The bio-toxicity of obtained CIP degrade solution using CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 after 5 min of photocatalytic reaction, was elaborated from the growth of E. coli in the degraded solution, as shown in Fig. 10 . The control experiments using water (Fig. 10b) and CIP solution (Fig. 10c) were conducted to compare the toxicity of the mineralized CIP solution. The occurrence of blue dot color after incubation indicates the formation of the colonies caused by the growth of E. coli. Compered, water (control experiment), the blue dot referring to E. coli colony in the MC-media pad was observed, thereby indicating that the experimental condition is suitable for E. coli growing. However, the presence of CIP inhibited the growth of E. coli because CIP is an antibiotic drug that is used to treat a variety of bacterial infections. Therefore, no blue dot was observed in the MC-media. Clearly, it can be observed that the blue dot colony of E. coli on the MC-media pad when E. coli was added with the degraded solution of CIP from using CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 as a photocatalyst. This indicates that the photocatalytic treatment of CIP using CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 reduce the bio-hazardous of the final solution by eliminating quinolone, fluorine, and piperazine ring from the CIP molecule, which are the fragments of the CIP structure that provide antibacterial potency to CIP (Picó and Andreu, 2007).

Based on the above discussions, Scheme 4 can be proposed as a possible mechanism for photocatalytic CIP degradation using CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50. From the Eg and electronic positions (Figs. 2b and 9c), the VB and CB positions of BiOBr are estimated to 1.64 eV and −1.28 eV, while the VB and CB positions of saponite are 2.77 eV and −1.20 eV. Under visible-light irradiation, the electron–hole pairs are generated in BiOBr. The photogenerated electron can transfer to CB and leave a hole in the VB. Moreover, the photogenerated electron from the CB of BiOBr can migrate to the CQDs and saponite through its heterojunctions to avoid the recombination of electron–hole pairs in BiOBr (Chen et al., 2016a, 2016b, 2020). The electron on the surface of the CQDs and clay can react with O2 in water to generate •O2 − because the electron has more negative energy than the standard reduction potential of O2 to •O2 − conversion, (E0(O2/•O2 −) = −0.046 eV versus NHE (Di et al., 2015). Simultaneously, some of the generated •O2 − moieties can also react with H+ and e− to generate •OH. Moreover, the hole in the VB of BiOBr can produce •OH and directly decompose the CIP molecules. The heterojunction in CQD/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 promotes the production of the three active species that are responsible for the CIP degradation under visible-light irradiation.

In summary, CQDs/Clay@BiOBr composites with excellent visible-light absorbance ability, charge separation efficiency, and photocatalytic performance were successfully synthesized using a hydrothermal method. Optimum performance was obtained for CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50, which showed two times higher photocatalytic activity than pure BiOBr. The different preparation methods of CQDs/Clay (in-situ synthesis and physical mixing) were found to affect the photocatalytic activity of the composites—CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 and CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50. The lower photocatalytic performance of CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr-50 than that of CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 may be due to the instability of CQDs/Clay (PM) and excess reconstruction of the CQDs on the surface of BiOBr, which reduces the light absorption ability and generation of reactive species. CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr-50 exhibited the best photocatalytic performance for photocatalytic degradation of CIP, compared with any other previously reported photocatalysts. The PL, photocurrent response, and EIS results indicated that the introduction of clay and CQDs in the Clay@BiOBr, CQDs/Clay (IS)@BiOBr and CQDs/Clay (PM)@BiOBr composites generate a heterojunction between BiOBr and the CQDs/Clay, thereby improving charge separation and charge transfer. This improvement in the charge separation and charge transfer enhanced the photocatalytic activity for CIP degradation. The saponite not only acted as a temple for good dispersion of nano-sized CQDs and 2D-layered BiOBr, but also contributed to the suppression of photogenerated charge recombination in the composite, resulting in the increased generation of active radicals, which enhanced the photocatalytic degradation of CIP under visible light irradiation. The degraded products after photocatalysis showed lower bio-toxicity than the original CIP solution, as confirmed by the E. coli test. This work provides a new design and fabrication method for CQDs-decorated clay composites that can be used as photocatalysts for wastewater under visible-light irradiation.
